summary:
The Velvet Revolution was the nonviolent transition from communist rule in Czechoslovakia that took place in November and December 1989. It began with student protests in Prague on 17 November 1989 — the fiftieth anniversary of a Nazi massacre of Czech students — which were violently suppressed by riot police. The brutal response galvanized public opinion, and within days hundreds of thousands of people were gathering in Prague's Wenceslas Square in protest.

The communist government, lacking the will to use large-scale violence and witnessing the collapse of communist regimes across Eastern Europe following the [[event:2681|fall of the Berlin Wall]], rapidly disintegrated. Playwright and dissident Václav Havel, who had led the Civic Forum opposition movement, became president in December 1989, and free elections were held in June 1990. The peaceful nature of the transition gave it the name Velvet Revolution.

The revolution was notable for the speed with which an apparently stable communist system collapsed and for the extraordinary moral authority of its leaders, particularly Havel, whose writings on living in truth under totalitarianism had made him one of the most respected dissidents in the world. Czechoslovakia subsequently peacefully dissolved into the Czech Republic and Slovakia on 1 January 1993, an event sometimes called the Velvet Divorce.
